    1. [How to represent a PTM (post translational modification)](#how-to-represent-a-ptm-\(post-translational-modification\))

    3. [How to represent molecular adaptors](#how-to-annotate-molecular-adaptors)

    5. [How to represent E3 ligase complexes](#how-to-annotate-e3-ubiquitin-ligases)  
    6. [How to represent transcription factors](#how-to-represent-transcription-factors)  
    7. [Nuclear receptors and ligand-activated transcription factors](#nuclear-receptors-and-ligand-activated-transcription-factors)  
    8. [How to deal with complexes](#how-to-represent-the-activities-of-complexes-in-go-cam)  
    9. [How to represent transporters](#how-to-represent-the-activities-of-complexes-in-go-cam)						

### **What is the aim of GO-CAM?**

GO-CAM (Gene Ontology Causal Activity Modeling) is designed to represent:

1\) ‘**activity units**’ that specify how different GO annotations (MF, BP, CC) for a given gene product relate to each other

2\) ‘**pathways**’ that specify the flow of causal connections between the activities of different gene products, to accomplish or regulate a larger biological process.

**GO-CAM: a complementary tool to standard GO annotation.**

Standard annotations refer to the connection established between a gene and a biological concept within the three aspects of the Gene Ontology (GO): Molecular Function (MF), Biological Process (BP), and Cellular Component (CC). These annotations consistently incorporate a reference, which can be a published, peer-reviewed paper or an internal GO reference, along with an evidence code that describes the experimental approach or method employed to formulate the statement. Although standard GO annotations offer valuable insights into fundamental gene information, they only present a limited perspective on the role of each gene within a larger biological framework.

To achieve a more comprehensive annotation of genes and establish causal relationships between their activities, the GO introduced GO-CAMs. By employing relations, GO-CAMs interlink GO annotations with biological entities and external ontology terms, thereby creating models that elucidate how genes function within the broader context of a biological process or pathway.

**Scope of GO-CAM models in SWISS-PROT:**

The Swiss-Prot annotation pipeline involves a comprehensive process of updating entries through extensive literature analysis and integrating the gathered data into Swiss-Prot and Protein2GO for standard Gene Ontology (GO) annotation. The inclusion of GO-CAM models in this pipeline serves as a valuable tool aimed at complementing and expanding upon standard GO annotations.

In Swiss-Prot, protein functions are described in a free-text format, encompassing all pertinent and useful information. Concurrently, standard GO annotation enhances this work by incorporating specific controlled vocabulary. The goal of GO-CAM models, for the curators, is to provide contextual information within standard GO annotations that cannot currently be represented in Protein2GO. For instance, GO-CAM enables the linking of a protein-mediated molecular activity to its subcellular location or a specific biological process.

Therefore, Swiss-Prot curators are encouraged to consider the advantages of creating a GO-CAM model while curating proteins of interest. In some cases, it may not be necessary to generate a model, and standard GO annotation would suffice to describe the data. However, when multiple activities are interconnected with specific contextual information that the curator wishes to depict, it becomes relevant and worthwhile to develop a GO-CAM model.

## 

### **Getting started with NOCTUA (setup user account)** {#getting-started-with-noctua-(setup-user-account)}

Noctua is accessed via: [http://noctua.geneontology.org/](http://noctua.geneontology.org/)

While GO-CAMs can be browsed using Noctua, no annotations can be created or edited unless a user has a registered account. 

To create a new account, please email help@geneontology.org or enter a ticket on the helpdesk repo in GitHub, providing your **ORCID** and **GitHub account handle** (user name).

###  {#heading}

### 

### 

### 

### 

### **The Noctua Landing Page**

### 

* The Noctua landing page is the portal by which curators can browse or search and filter models.

* By default, the Noctua landing page displays models by date, descending order, i.e. the most recently edited models are shown at the top of the list.

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image1]

**Searching models:**Clicking on the magnifying glass opens or closes the menu of available filter options: 

1. **Ontology term** (autocomplete)  
2. **Gene product** (autocomplete)  
3. **Reference**   
4. **Organism** (drop-down list)  
5. **Contributor** (autocomplete and drop-down list)  
6. **Groups** (autocomplete and drop-down list)  
7. **Exact date** (enter YYYY-MM-DD/return or calendar, select/return) (![A yellow light bulb in a black backgroundDescription automatically generated][image2] not recommended, as this is not informative, since dates change even for automated updated)  
8. **Date range** (enter YYYY-MM-DD/return or calendar, select/return)  
9. **Title** (enter/return)  
10. **State** (drop-down list)

**Creating a model:**

### 

Noctua has three curation interfaces. T: 1\) the [Pathway Editor](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1RNbr-2T6CSZfaYKDrom7259IK6ck82vZLThgymyNZ28), 2\) the Noctua Form and 3\) the Noctua Graph Editor. 

**The [Pathway Editor](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1RNbr-2T6CSZfaYKDrom7259IK6ck82vZLThgymyNZ28) (VPE), described in this document, is designed for curating GO-CAM models, i.e. models that include at least two activities/MFs linked by causal relations.** The other interfaces are used for other purposes:

* The Noctua Form is recommended for creating 'standard' GO annotations.  
* The Noctua Graph Editor is an old tool for creating GO-CAM models and should not be used for creating new models

### 

 **Opening an existing model:**

To open an existing model, there are two options:

1. Click on the Pathway editor icon ![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image3]  
2. Click on the Form editor icon  ![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image4]

### **The copy model functionality**

‘Model Copy’ gives curators the ability to copy an existing (source) model to use as a starting point to create a new model.  Information copied from a source model includes:

* Genes or gene products (annotatable objects)   
* Ontology terms  
* Relationships to and between ontology terms   
* Additional data such as inputs and outputs   
* Evidence (evidence codes \+ references) when this option is selected by the curator.

This functionality plays a crucial role when the curator intends to depict the regulation of a particular process or to capture the same pathway in a different cell type. Any model can be duplicated and modified as needed.

Model Copy functionality can be accessed from:

* Noctua Landing Page

 

1. Click on the extra menu (at the right of the model), choose ‘Copy Model’  
   2. Check the ‘Include Evidence’ box if evidences from the original model should be kept   
   3. Click on the Copy Model button  
      

![A yellow light bulb in a black backgroundDescription automatically generated][image2] If you change the evidences for an ‘activity unit’, don’t forget to check the evidences for the causal relations linked to the ‘activity unit’.

* Pathway Editor


From the Pathway Editor, first open the model.  To access the Model Copy functionality, click on the third icon after the model title

### **Getting started with the Pathway Editor (VPE)** {#getting-started-with-the-pathway-editor-(vpe)}

Introduction

The Pathway Editor is designed for curating GO-CAM models, i.e.  
models that include at least two activities/MFs, and causal relations between them  
like “directly positively regulates”. For standard annotations or models with no causal  
relations, we recommend using the Noctua Form interface or Protein2GO.  
In the pathway editor, there are three basic building blocks for creating a pathway:

∙ ![A diagram of a activity unitDescription automatically generated][image5] An “activity unit”

∙ ![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image6] A protein complex 

∙ ![A white and blue hexagon with black textDescription automatically generated][image7] A chemical entity (for metabolic pathways, or to show regulatory effects of a  
small molecule on a GP)

The building blocks are then connected into causal chains by clicking and dragging  
from one block to another in the direction of causal flow. The types of building blocks  
and directionality are detected automatically by the tool, and the user is given  
choices to help them select the appropriate causal relation.

Basic workflow

1\. Create an activity unit for each gene product in the pathway.

2\. Create a chemical for each reactant and product in the pathway (if necessary).

3\. Connect activities and/or chemicals into causal chains.

![A yellow light bulb in a black backgroundDescription automatically generated][image2] Hint: Models are saved automatically.

![A yellow light bulb in a black backgroundDescription automatically generated][image8] Hint: Models created with the Pathway Editor adhere to the GO-CAM specifications.

Pathway editor general layout  
![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image9]  
1\. The Model Title and State can be edited by clicking on the pen at the right.

2\. All new models have the state "Development" assigned by default. Once finished,  
mark the model as “Production” (click on “model status” shown in next slide). It  
will be released publicly in the next GOC release cycle but can still be edited  
and updated at any time and released again. Models can also be deleted from here.

3\. and 4\. Date and contributors are added automatically.

5\. The toolbox.

**Activity unit** This creates a new activity for a GENE PRODUCT. An activity unit is defined by a gene product, its molecular activity (MF), location where its is active (MF occurs\_in CC), molecular target of the activity (MF has\_input gene product or chemical), biological process the activity is part of (MF part\_of BP).

**Protein complex**: This creates a new activity for a PROTEIN COMPLEX. This is the same as the “Activity unit” except that the activity is carried out by a protein complex instead of a single gene product. **This should rarely be used** (see [guidelines below for protein complexes](#how-to-represent-the-activities-of-complexes-in-go-cam)).  
**Chemical**: This creates a new SMALL MOLECULE or ion on the canvas. This serves to link metabolic pathways via intermediates or to represent chemical ligands, for example.

6 and 7\. Layout detail.

Various levels of details can be visualized in the Pathway editor canvas. 

1. Default: shows all activity unit content except evidences  
   2. Simple view: hides output and happens during  
   1. Preview: only shows gene names/chemicals for each node

## Navigation 

1. Click on the 'Gene Ontology' logo to go to the [Gene Ontology Website](http://geneontology.org/).  
2. Workbenches menu (see below).  
3. Click on 'Noctua' in the page header to go back to the Noctua Landing Page.  
4. 'About' has links to general information about the [GO](http://geneontology.org/docs/introduction-to-go-resource/) and about [GO-CAM](http://geneontology.org/docs/gocam-overview/).   
5. 'Help' leads to the general [Noctua User Guide](https://wiki.geneontology.org/index.php/Noctua).  
6. Clicking on the Alliance of Genomes' logo leads to the [Alliance Website](https://www.alliancegenome.org/).

### 2\. Workbenches menu

The workbench provides a menu to access various tools:

\-Alliance pathway preview displays the model the way it will be represented in the alliancegenome.org website.

\-Annotation preview provides all standard GO annotation generated from the GO-CAM model.

\-Cytoscape layout mode displays only the molecules with their activities and the links to the other activities present in the model.

\-Other available tools are used for QC and software development

### **Creating a Molecular Activity Unit in VPE**

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image10]

1\. Click on activity unit,  
2\. Hold mouse button to drag the unit on the workbench,  
3\. Releasing the button opens up an Activity Unit form.

The Activity Unit form contains :

1\.   The gene product (UniProt accession number ex: Q14653 or gene name followed by hsap)

* For mouse proteins use MGI accession, and for other MODs use their accession numbers.

2\.   The Molecular Function GO term (MF).  
3\.   The Biological Process GO term (BP).  
4\.   The Cellular Component GO term (CC).  
5\.   The evidence code, ex: IDA for ‘direct assay evidence used in manual assertion’.  
6\.   The reference, ex PMID:123123.  
7\.   The ‘with’ box is used for similarity evidences, with UniProtKB:Q14658 (primary annotation).  
8\.   The save button to save the activity unit.

Using an existing annotation in a model

It is possible to use already existing annotations in a model. 

1. In the Activity Unit, click on the 'More action' menu ![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image11] on the right, and select the first option, 'Search annotations'  
2. A pop-up will open, listing all existing annotations available for the selected gene.  
3. When an annotation is selected, a green checkbox appears on the left, and evidences are displayed  
4. Selecting one of the evidences will load it in the form  
5. Save to close the form by pushing Done.

## Adding a chemical: Molecule Form

## 

1. In the Toolbox on the left, click on the Chemical box.  
2. Hold mouse button to drag the unit on the workbench.  
3. Releasing the button opens up a Molecule form.

* In the Molecule Form, it is possible to add a location for the molecule, and evidence for that location. This information is optional.

Chemicals are displayed in the Pathway editor as follows: 

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image12] 

* For chemicals, we use Chemical Entities of Biological Interest (ChEBI). Curators should try to use the same ChEBI as RHEA whenever possible.

### **The components of a molecular activity unit**

The main info are:

Molecular Function 

In GO-CAM, the molecular function term employed should accurately describe the protein's activity during the specific process depicted in the model. We refrain from using Molecular Function binding terms (e.g., GO:0035325, Toll-like receptor binding) as they do not precisely reflect the protein's actual activity. Whenever possible, there should be one activity unit per gene product, and the detailed mechanism description should be in the definition of the MF term. For example, a tyrosine protein kinase receptor’s activity could in principle be broken down into ligand binding, followed by autophosphorylation, followed by recruitment of signaling complex components (binding). However, for GO-CAM this is not ideal, as it would introduce complexity that is better represented by selecting a single MF term (GO:0004714, transmembrane receptor tyrosine protein kinase activity), or more specific child term). If a multi-step mechanism for the same gene product is not currently available in the ontology, a new term should be requested. In some cases, distinct catalytic activities (different EC or Rhea identifiers) are carried out by distinct domains of a protein, e.g. subsequent steps in a biochemical pathway. In these cases distinct activities for the same product should be used. 

Inputs

The molecular activity often acts upon a specific entity, referred to as the ‘input’ (this is generally the substrate or target of the activity). The entity captured must have a unique, resolvable database identifier. As with annotation objects, gene identifiers may be used as a stand-in for a gene product, e.g. an mRNA or a protein. For instance, in the case of a kinase, the input would be its substrate, as the kinase's activity acts on this substrate. Similarly, for a ligand, the input would be its receptor, as the ligand acts on the receptor (note that the ligand is not the input of its receptor, as causal activity models are directional). By including the input in the molecular unit, GO-CAM effectively captures the key elements and interactions involved in the molecular activities. To add an input on the activity unit, navigate to the 'More actions' menu ![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image13] located on the right side of the MF level. From there, select the second option, 'Add,' and then choose 'Add has Input’.

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image14]

Outputs

In GO-CAM, the ‘output’ is used to specify the specific molecular output produced as a result of a Molecular Function. This is usually the product of a reaction corresponding to the MF. The entity captured must have a unique, resolvable database identifier. As with the active entity that enables the MF, gene identifiers may be used as a stand-in for an output that represents a gene product, e.g. an mRNA or a protein. To add an output on the activity unit, simply navigate to the 'More actions' menu ![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image13] located on the right side of the MF level. From there, select the second option, 'Add,' and then choose 'Add has Output’

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image15]

Biological Process

In GO-CAM, each activity should be associated with the biological process (BP) it is a part of. As models are usually centered on a main biological process, most activities in a model will refer to the same GO BP term. However, there are many cases where different BP terms will be included in the same model, e.g. activities that specifically regulate an activity in the pathway (and a regulation of BP term might be used instead), or to connect to upstream and downstream pathways.  Whenever a model depicts a pathway, the corresponding GO biological process must be present in all relevant activity units. Moreover, the activity unit provides the flexibility to incorporate multiple, nested biological processes. To achieve this, simply navigate to the 'More actions' menu ![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image13] located on the right side of the BP level. From there, select the second option, 'Add,' and then choose 'Add part of BP.' This way, you can link broader (cellular and organism-level) biological processes to  molecular processes such as signaling and metabolic pathways.

For example: an E3 ligase performs a Lys-63-mediated ubiquitination during cytosolic pattern recognition receptor signaling pathway. In that case, we have two BPs starting with **the most specific one being part of the more general one**.

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image16]

Cellular Component

Cellular component annotations are intended to capture where the gene product (or, rarely, the complex) is located when it performs its Molecular Function s. For instance, if the activity is nuclear, the GO:0005634 nucleus is used.  For signaling receptors, the cellular component is often GO:0005886 (plasma membrane) or a more specific child term. Cellular component annotations may be further qualified with cell, tissue, and organismal context if that information is relevant to the process being modeled. In that case, simply navigate to the 'More actions' menu ![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image13] located on the right side of the CC level. From there, select the second option, 'Add,' and then choose 'Add part of CC’, or cell type, anatomy, if required.

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image17]

###  {#heading-1}

### 

### 

### **The available causal relations between molecular activity units**

Here are the available Causal Relations in GO-CAM to link activities to one another (MF to MF):

| directly negatively regulates  | Links two activities when the upstream activity has a negative causal effect (decreasing or inhibiting) on an immediately downstream activity. Immediately means there is no intervening activity. The mechanism by which the upstream activity controls the downstream activity must be known. ![A blue and black textDescription automatically generated][image18] |
| :---- | :---- |
| directly positively regulates  | Links two activities when the upstream activity has a positive causal effect (increasing or activating) on an immediately downstream activity. Immediately means there is no intervening activity. The mechanism by which the upstream activity controls the downstream activity must be known. ![A blue and black textDescription automatically generated][image18] |
| causally upstream of, negative effect  | Links two activities when the upstream activity has a negative causal effect (decreasing or inhibiting) on the downstream activity but the mechanism is not known. Click on GO for examples  ![A blue and black textDescription automatically generated][image18] |
| causally upstream of, positive effect  | Links two activities when the upstream activity has a positive causal effect (increasing or activating) on the downstream activity but the mechanism is not known. ![A blue and black textDescription automatically generated][image18] |
| provides input for  | Links two successive activities when the product (output) of the upstream activity is the substrate (input) for the downstream activity, and the product is a macromolecule (i.e. DNA, RNA, protein). ![A blue and black textDescription automatically generated][image18] |
| removes input for  | Links two activities when the upstream activity has a negative causal effect (decreasing or inhibiting) on the downstream activity and the two activities act on or modify the same molecular target at the same site(s). ![A blue and black textDescription automatically generated][image18] |
| constitutively upstream of  | Links two activities when the upstream activity is required for the downstream activity, but does not regulate the downstream activity. ![A blue and black textDescription automatically generated][image18] |
| indirectly negatively regulates  | Links two activities when the upstream activity has a negative regulatory effect (decreasing or inhibiting) on the downstream activity via a larger process (e.g. proteasome-mediated protein degradation) that is reused in many contexts and the curator does not want to reproduce that process in the GO-CAM. The mechanism by which the upstream activity controls the downstream activity must be known. ![A blue and black textDescription automatically generated][image18] |
| indirectly positively regulates  | Links two activities when the upstream activity has a positive regulatory effect (increasing or activating) on the downstream activity via a larger process (e.g. transcription) that is reused in many contexts and the curator does not want to reproduce that process in the GO-CAM. The mechanism by which the upstream activity controls the downstream activity must be known. ![A blue and black textDescription automatically generated][image18] |

| is small molecule activator of  | Links a small molecule and an activity, when the small molecule activates the activity. ![A blue and black textDescription automatically generated][image18] |
| :---- | :---- |
| is small molecule inhibitor of  | Links a small molecule and an activity, when the small molecule inhibits the activity. ![A blue and black textDescription automatically generated][image18] |

In the Pathway Editor, activity units are connected by dragging the left-mouse button pressed from one activity to the next, which opens a menu with all the previously described relations.

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image19]

### **How to annotate models with evidence from different organisms** {#how-to-annotate-models-with-evidence-from-different-organisms}

### 

We curate GO-CAM models within a single species. For the time being Swiss-Prot is focusing on human centric models. Nevertheless, instances may arise where multiple pieces of evidence originate from alternative organisms, such as mice or rats. In such situations, models are constructed using exclusively human protein accession numbers, with evidence from other organisms incorporated through similarity. If, however, all evidence is derived from mice and the mechanism described is species-specific, a mouse model can be considered. If possible, these MFs should be added using the ‘Search annotations’ functionality, locating an annotation with the IBA evidence code. When the desired term is not currently available using this functionality, curators should: 1\) create a ticket in the GO annotation repo at https://github.com/geneontology/go-annotation/issues (remember to add the ‘PAINT’ label on the issue) to improve the PAINT/PAN-GO annotations, and (2) create a new ‘by similarity’ (ISS) annotation in the GO-CAM model.

Procedure to create an annotation ‘By similarity’ in GO-CAM

1\-In the evidence field, the ISS evidence code is used

2\- In the reference box, insert GO\_REF:0000024

3\- In the with field, use the accession number of the gene/protein that has an experimental annotation to the selected GO term, e.g. a MOD accession number (Ex: MGI:109275)

IMPORTANT: If no experimental (primary) annotation currently exists, the primary standard annotation *must* be made. It can be made in Noctua or in another tool, such as Protein2GO.

### **How to represent a PTM (post translational modification)**  {#how-to-represent-a-ptm-(post-translational-modification)}

PTMs are not represented explicitly. Instead, they are implied by the molecular function (MF) term, typically the enzymatic activity  resulting in the post-translational modification.

The **Molecular Function** represents the type of reaction by which the substrate is modified (ex: protein serine/threonine kinase activity)

### The **‘has input’** corresponds to the substrate of the protein activity. 

### This model captures the fact that a phosphate group is added to IRF3. It is not currently possible to specify the exact residue(s) that are phosphorylated.

### Note: the input should be added to the molecular unit so it can be exported as extension for the standard GO annotation generated for the model.

###  {#heading-2}

### 

### 

### 

### **How to represent a signaling receptor activity**

Receptor activation by a ligand is represented differently in GO-CAM depending on whether the ligand is (i) a protein (i. e., encoded by a gene) or (ii) a small molecule. 

## *1- Protein ligand-activated signaling receptor*

### **Ligand**

The activity unit for a ligand of a signaling receptor is:

**\-MF**: 	a ligand 'enables' receptor ligand activity ([GO:0048018](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0048018)) or a child

**\-Input: **The input of the ligand is the molecule its acts on, so the receptor in that case

**\-BP** '	part of' the process in which the ligand participates, usually a child of signal transduction ([GO:0007165](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0007165))

**\-CC: 	\-**extracellular ligands: 'occurs in' extracellular space ([GO:0005615](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0005886))  
\-membrane-bound ligands: 'occurs in' plasma membrane ([GO:0005886](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0005886))

The causal relation between the **ligand activity** and the **receptor activity** is   
'directly positively regulates'.

### **Signaling receptor**

The activity unit for a signaling receptor is:

**\-MF:** 'enables' signaling receptor activity ([GO:0038023](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0038023)*)* or a child*.* 

**\-Input** (target): of the receptor is the **effector protein it regulates**, for example a molecular adaptor, captured with the *'*has input' relation.

* **Note that the input (target) of the receptor is NOT its ligand **


**\-BP** 'part of' the same BP/signal transduction pathway as the ligand

**\-CC:** transmembrane receptors: 'occurs in' plasma membrane ([GO:0005886](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0005886))

* The causal relation between the MF of the **receptor** and the MF of its **target** is 'directly positively regulates

**Example: [Insulin signaling model](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A6482692800000931) **  
![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image20]

## *2- Small molecule-activated signaling receptor activity*

* Since small molecules are not annotated in GO, a small molecule ligand does not have a molecular function. Instead, the **ligand** and the **receptor activity** are linked by the causal relation 'is small molecule activator'*.*  
* The receptor's function, input, and contextual relations are the same as for protein ligand-activated receptors.   
* Note that it is also possible to annotate an inhibitory ligand using the relation 'is small molecule inhibitor'*.*  
* BP and CC annotations are the same as for protein ligand-activated receptor activity.

**Example: [Activation of a GPCR by succinate](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A648d0dc100000022)**

![A screenshot of a cellDescription automatically generated][image21]

## Special cases

### Receptor with coreceptor 

This is common in immune receptors. The typical sequence of events is that the ligand binds the signaling receptor, which signals to the co-receptor to activate its downstream effector (such as a protein kinase).   
	

**Example: [IL2 signaling pathway (Human)](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel:6205c24300001663)**  
In this case, the receptor is activated by an information biomacromolecule (i.e. a protein). Interleukin-2 ([IL2](https://www.uniprot.org/uniprot/P60568)), a cytokine, activates its receptor, interleukin-2 receptor A ([IL2RA](https://www.uniprot.org/uniprot/P01589)).   
IL2RA directly positively regulates (activates) [IL2RB](https://www.uniprot.org/uniprot/P14784), which phosphorylates and positively regulates [JAK1](https://www.uniprot.org/uniprotkb/P23458).

The activity unit for a signaling coreceptor is:

**\-MF:** coreceptor activity ([GO:0015026](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0015026)*) *

**\-Input** (target): of the receptor is the **effector protein it regulates** which is the **signaling coreceptor** it regulates in that case, captured with the *'*has input' relation  
**Note that the input (target) of the coreceptor is NOT the ligand but the downstream component it regulates**

**\-BP** 'part of' the BP in which the signaling receptor is involved, usually a child of signal transduction ([GO:0007165](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0007165))

**\-CC:** transmembrane receptors: 'occurs in' plasma membrane ([GO:0005886](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0005886))

* The causal relation between the MF of the **receptor** and the MF of its **coreceptor** is 'directly positively regulates'*.*

![DiagramDescription automatically generated with medium confidence][image22]

Note: 

* cytokine activity is\_a receptor ligand activity  
* interleukin-2 receptor activity is\_a signaling receptor activity

### **How to annotate molecular adaptors** {#how-to-annotate-molecular-adaptors}

Here we describe how to capture a molecular adaptor activity which is defined as the binding activity of a molecule that brings together two or more molecules through a selective, non-covalent, often stoichiometric interaction, permitting those molecules to function in a coordinated way.

Example 1: [TYROBP acts as an adaptor between a receptor and a downstream effector](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A633b013300001197)

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated with medium confidence][image23]

The molecular activity unit for molecular adaptor may include:

o **MF**: molecular adaptor activity ([GO:0060090](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0060090)) or one of its children

o **Has input** the two (or more) molecules it brings together.

o **BP**: the BP it participates in 

o **CC**: the place where the activity occurs.

The relation with the downstream activity used is **“directly positively regulates”** 

Example 2: When an adaptor brings together an enzyme and its substrate

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated with medium confidence][image24]

The molecular activity is the same as above (MF, inputs, BP, CC)

But in that case, the relation with the downstream activity used is “**provides input for**”

### 

### **How to annotate sequestering proteins** {#how-to-annotate-sequestering-proteins}

Here we describe how to capture a sequestering activity which is defined as the binding to a specific molecule to prevent it from interacting with other partners or to inhibit its localization to the area of the cell or complex where it is active

Example 1: [Sequestering activity of CAV1 on TLR4 signaling](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A645d887900001414)

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated with medium confidence][image25]

The molecular activity unit for molecular adaptor may include:

o **MF**: molecular sequestering activity ([GO:0140313](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0140313)) or one of its children

o **Has input** the molecule it sequesters.

o **BP**: the BP it inhibits 

o **CC**: the place where the activity occurs.

The relation with the downstream activity used is **“directly negatively regulates”** 

Example 2: [Sequestering activity of YWHAZ on RAF1](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A63d320cd00000820)

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image26]

In that example, the difference in the cellular component between the two activities clearly show that the YWHAZ protein sequesters RAF1 in the cytoplasm, preventing its activity in the plasma membrane. 

The relation used between both activities is **“directly negatively regulates”** because there is direct interaction between both proteins.

### 

### **How to annotate E3 ubiquitin ligases** {#how-to-annotate-e3-ubiquitin-ligases}

1. An E3 ligase that promotes ubiquitination by itself

ex: [TRIM45-mediated degradation of TAB2 leading to inflammatory response inhibition (Human)](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A63f809ec00000307) 

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image27]

* The molecular activity unit for the ubiquitin ligase is:  
* **MF**: ubiquitin protein ligase activity [GO:0061630](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/GTerm?id=GO:0061630#term=history)   
* Has **input** the substrate protein  
* **BP**: if known, the **BP** should describe the type of ubiquitination (K-48, K-63…) 

  ex: protein K48-linked ubiquitination [GO:0070936](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0070936) 

  if not known, use a **BP** describing the biological process in which the ubiquitination is involved in.

  ex: proteasome-mediated ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process [GO:0043161](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/GTerm?id=GO:0043161#term=history) or children, such as: SCF-dependent proteasomal ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process ([GO:0031146](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0031146)) or ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process via the C-end degron rule pathway ([GO:0140627](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0140627))

  ‘Part of’ the **BP** regulated by this mechanism (ex: negative regulation of inflammatory response, [GO:0050728](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0050728))

* The causal relation to the substrate molecular activity unit is: ‘indirectly negatively regulates’. 

2. An E3-ligase complex with adaptors and scaffold protein(s) : 

* **When we know the substrate adaptor, scaffold(s), ligase and substrate**

Ex: [DCAF12 controls MOV10 during T cell activation. (Human)](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A636d9ce800001192)

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image28]

* The molecular activity unit for the ubiquitin ligase complex scaffold, such as CUL4A and/or DDB1 is:  
* **MF**: ubiquitin ligase complex scaffold activity [GO:0160072](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/GTerm?id=GO:0160072#term=history)   
* Has **input** both the ubiquitin ligase-substrate adaptor and the ubiquitin protein ligase  
* **BP**: if known, the **BP** should describe the type of ubiquitination (K-48, K-63…) 

  ex: protein K48-linked ubiquitination [GO:0070936](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0070936) 

  if not known, use a **BP** describing the biological process in which the ubiquitination is involved in.

  ex: proteasome-mediated ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process [GO:0043161](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/GTerm?id=GO:0043161#term=history) or children, such as: SCF-dependent proteasomal ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process ([GO:0031146](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0031146)) or ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process via the C-end degron rule pathway ([GO:0140627](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0140627))

  ‘Part of’ the BP regulated by this mechanism (ex: T cell activation, [GO:0042110](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0042110))

* The causal relation to the substrate molecular activity unit is: ‘directly negatively regulates’ if it leads to degradation. (to decide for the other cases) 

* The causal relation to the ubiquitin ligase-substrate adaptor molecular activity unit is: ‘directly regulates’. 

* The molecular activity unit for the ubiquitin ligase-substrate adaptor is:  
* **MF**: ubiquitin ligase-substrate adaptor activity [GO:1990756](http://amigo.geneontology.org/amigo/term/GO:1990756)   
* Has **input** the substrate protein  
* **BP**: if known, the **BP** should describe the type of ubiquitination (K-48, K-63…) 

  ex: protein K48-linked ubiquitination [GO:0070936](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0070936) 

  if not known, use a **BP** describing the biological process in which the ubiquitination is involved in.

  ex: proteasome-mediated ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process [GO:0043161](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/GTerm?id=GO:0043161#term=history) or children, such as: SCF-dependent proteasomal ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process ([GO:0031146](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0031146)) or ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process via the C-end degron rule pathway ([GO:0140627](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0140627))

  ‘Part of’ the BP regulated by this mechanism (ex: T cell activation, [GO:0042110](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0042110))

* The causal relation to the ubiquitin ligase molecular activity unit is: ‘directly provides input for’.

* The molecular activity unit for the ubiquitin ligase is:  
* **MF**: ubiquitin protein ligase activity [GO:0061630](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/GTerm?id=GO:0061630#term=history)   
* Has **input** the substrate protein  
* **BP**: if known, the **BP** should describe the type of ubiquitination (K-48, K-63…) 

  ex: protein K48-linked ubiquitination [GO:0070936](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0070936) 

  if not known, use a **BP** describing the biological process in which the  ubiquitination is involved in.

  ex: proteasome-mediated ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process [GO:0043161](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/GTerm?id=GO:0043161#term=history) or children, such as: SCF-dependent proteasomal ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process ([GO:0031146](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0031146)) or ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process via the C-end degron rule pathway ([GO:0140627](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0140627))

  ‘Part of’ the BP regulated by this mechanism (ex: T cell activation, [GO:0042110](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0042110))

* The causal relation to the substrate molecular activity unit is: ‘indirectly negatively regulates’.

* **When we don’t know all the proteins involved in the process, but we know the general mechanism (e.g. proteasomal degradation):**

* Only substrate adaptor and substrate:

  Ex: [FBXL19-mediated degradation of IL1R1 via GSK3B (Human)](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A63d320cd00001588)![A diagram of a computerDescription automatically generated with medium confidence][image29]


  

* The molecular activity unit for the ubiquitin ligase-substrate adaptor is:  
* **MF**: ubiquitin ligase-substrate adaptor activity [GO:1990756](http://amigo.geneontology.org/amigo/term/GO:1990756)   
* Has **input** the substrate protein  
* **BP**: if known, the **BP** should describe the type of ubiquitination (K-48, K-63…) 

  ex: protein K48-linked ubiquitination [GO:0070936](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0070936) 

  if not known, use a **BP** describing the biological process in which the  ubiquitination is involved in.

  ex: proteasome-mediated ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process [GO:0043161](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/GTerm?id=GO:0043161#term=history) or children, such as: SCF-dependent proteasomal ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process ([GO:0031146](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0031146)) or ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process via the C-end degron rule pathway ([GO:0140627](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0140627))

  ‘Part of’ the BP regulated by this mechanism (ex: T cell activation, [GO:0042110](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0042110))

* The causal relation to the substrate molecular activity unit is: ‘indirectly regulates’ since we don’t add/have information about the E3 ligase.

* Only substrate adaptor, scaffold and substrate:

Ex: [DCAF13 supports the spindle assembly and chromosome condensation during oocyte meiotic division by targeting PTEN polyubiquitination and degradation. (Human)](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A63c0ac2b00001634)

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image30]

* The molecular activity unit for the ubiquitin ligase complex scaffold, such as CUL4A and/or DDB1 is:  
* **MF**: ubiquitin ligase complex scaffold activity [GO:0160072](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/GTerm?id=GO:0160072#term=history)   
* Has **input** both the ubiquitin ligase-substrate adaptor and the ubiquitin protein ligase  
* **BP**: if known, the **BP** should describe the type of ubiquitination (K-48, K-63…) 

  ex: protein K48-linked ubiquitination [GO:0070936](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0070936) 

  if not known, use a **BP** describing the biological process in which the  ubiquitination is involved in.

  ex: proteasome-mediated ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process [GO:0043161](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/GTerm?id=GO:0043161#term=history) or children, such as: SCF-dependent proteasomal ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process ([GO:0031146](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0031146)) or ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process via the C-end degron rule pathway ([GO:0140627](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0140627))

  ‘Part of’ the BP regulated by this mechanism (ex: T cell activation, [GO:0042110](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0042110))

* The causal relation to the ubiquitin ligase-substrate adaptor molecular activity unit is: ‘directly regulates’. 

* The molecular activity unit for the ubiquitin ligase-substrate adaptor is:  
* **MF**: ubiquitin ligase-substrate adaptor activity [GO:1990756](http://amigo.geneontology.org/amigo/term/GO:1990756)   
* Has **input** the substrate protein  
* **BP**: if known, the **BP** should describe the type of ubiquitination (K-48, K-63…) 

  ex: protein K48-linked ubiquitination [GO:0070936](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0070936) 

  if not known, use a **BP** describing the biological process in which the  ubiquitination is involved in.

  ex: proteasome-mediated ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process [GO:0043161](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/GTerm?id=GO:0043161#term=history) or children, such as: SCF-dependent proteasomal ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process ([GO:0031146](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0031146)) or ubiquitin-dependent protein catabolic process via the C-end degron rule pathway ([GO:0140627](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0140627))

  ‘Part of’ the BP regulated by this mechanism (ex: T cell activation, [GO:0042110](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0042110))

* The causal relation to the substrate molecular activity unit is: ‘indirectly regulates’ since we don’t add/have information about the E3 ligase.

### 

### **How to represent transcription factors** {#how-to-represent-transcription-factors}

### DNA-binding transcription factor activity \- Single transcription target

The activity unit for a eukaryotic DNA-binding transcription factor is:

* **MF:** 'enables' a child of DNA binding transcription factor activity, RNA polymerase, II-specific ([GO:0000981](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0000981)):  
  * DNA-binding transcription activator activity, RNA polymerase, II-specific ([GO:0001228](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0001228))  
  * DNA-binding transcription repressor activity, RNA polymerase II-specific ([GO:0001227](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0001227))


* **Context: **  
  * The relation between the DNA-binding transcription factor activity and the gene it regulates is 'has input' 

* **BP: '**part of' **regulation of the BP** in which the target participates (if known)**.**  
* **CC:** 'occurs in' nucleus ([GO:0005634](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0005634))  
* The causal relation between the transcription factor activity and the activity of its target gene is: ‘indirectly positively regulates’ or 'indirectly negatively regulates’, since there are many steps between the activation of transcription and the activity of the target protein, including the production of a messenger RNA that is translated into a protein, i. e the regulator does not directly interact with the protein it regulates.

* 

**Example single target: [FOXO3 regulation of G6PC1](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A645d887900001840)**

### DNA-binding transcription factor activity \- Multiple transcription targets

In cases where transcription factor regulates multiple target genes, a separate activity unit is captured for each transcriptional target.

**Example multiple targets: [FOXO3 regulation of G6PC1 and Pck1](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A645d887900001840)**

### 

### 

### 

### 

### 

### 

### 

### 

### 

### 

### 

### 

### **Nuclear receptors and ligand-activated transcription factors** {#nuclear-receptors-and-ligand-activated-transcription-factors}

### 

### 

* Nuclear receptors are positively regulated by a ligand, usually a small molecule (ChEBI). 

* The activity unit for a nuclear receptor is:  
    
  * **MF**: nuclear receptor activity ([GO:0004879](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/GTerm?id=GO:0004879)) (a child of transcription factor activity)  
      
  * **Context:** the causal relation between the small molecule and the nuclear receptor is ‘is small activator of’.


    

  * Other data are captured the same way as for [other transcription factors](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1o06rwrGwx8Fw_fqw3ldsrGlM0ot21Q137yQ95ySpasc/edit#heading=h.fbcjfrmmj04i).

**Example: [Model for nuclear receptor annotation](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A6482692800001263)**

### **How to represent the activities of complexes in GO-CAM** {#how-to-represent-the-activities-of-complexes-in-go-cam}

Curators should always strive to assign an MF to an individual gene or gene product.

Protein-containing complexes are used as enablers ***only*** when they enable an emergent function, i.e. none of the subunits is capable physiologically, or individually has the active sites, to perform the function. If a curator can assign the MF to an individual member of the complex, *do not use* the whole complex as the enabler, use the gene or gene product.  A second case in which a protein-containing complex may be used as an enabler is when the activities of the individual subunits are not known so thus cannot confidently be ascribed to a single member.

Only protein-containing complex (GO:0032991) or a child term will be allowed as an enabler in a GO-CAM model, and these should be as specific as possible.  If a specific complex term is not currently in GO, it is okay to use GO:0032991 while curating, but curators are *encouraged* to request a more specific GO protein-containing complex term from the ontology editors (make a new ticket at [https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues](https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues)) and update models when the new term becomes available.  Important\! It is not necessary to list all the complex components. The curator selects those most relevant for the annotation.

1\-Drag a “protein complex” box to the centre of the page to open a protein complex form

2\-Click on the \+ menu

3\-Click on “has part” to add a component to the complex

* **Output in GPAD**

Individual gene products that are parts of an enabling complex will be output as standard annotations in the GPAD with a ‘contributes to’ gene product-to-term relation to the MF enabled by the complex in the GO-CAM.  (Note that the addition of ‘contributes to’ to the GPAD export from GO-CAMs has not yet been implemented in the GO release pipeline, but will be available in the near future.)  Also, gene products will be ‘part of’ the protein-containing complex (or child).

### **How to represent transporters**	

The activity unit for a transmembrane transporter is:

* **MF**: 'enables' a child of  transmembrane transporter activity [GO:0022857](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0022857)  
* **Context: **  
  * The movement of the small molecule substrate is represented by:   
    * small molecule (ChEBI) \+ ‘input of’ \+ the start location of the small molecule, captured with the relation 'located in'  
    * the transporter activity \+ ‘has output’ the small molecule (ChEBI) \+ the end location of the small molecule, captured with the relation 'located in'  
  * **BP** 'part of' the BP in which this transporter activity participates  
  * **CC** 'occurs in' a child of membrane ([GO:0016020](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0016020)), e. g.: lysosomal membrane ([GO:0005765](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0005765)).

**Example: [SLC17A9 transports ATP to the lysosomal lumen](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A63f809ec00001779)**

![A screenshot of a computerDescription automatically generated][image31]
